summary: @thymelab/logfx is a consola-impersonating dropper that fetches and executes encrypted remote payloads on first logger use
details: The package is published as @thymelab/logfx but its package.json repository/homepage and README impersonate the well-known unjs/consola logger. The shipped dist/logfx.js wraps the exported withTag() method with a hidden IIFE that, on first invocation, calls require('./bootstrap').runPrepare(). dist/bootstrap.js is a 282 KB obfuscator.io-packed bundle (1267-entry RC4 string array, control-flow flattening, debugger/self-defense and constructor-toString anti-prettify checks) whose deobfuscated logic AES-256-GCM-decrypts a list of hardcoded HTTPS URLs from XORed byte tables, performs an HTTPS GET on each URL, writes the response to a temp file, and then spawns the fetched payload with the local Node binary (child_process.spawn(process.execPath, [tmpFile], {detached:true, stdio:'ignore'}).unref()) or via bash -c on non-Windows hosts, caching by sha256 manifest. Any consumer that calls the package's documented API (e.g. logfx.withTag('api')) immediately triggers attacker-controlled remote code execution on the installer's machine. The obfuscation, the typosquat-by-impersonation of consola, and the smuggled hook into a public logger method establish deliberate supply-chain attack intent.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
